The canopy goes quiet just before a black shape drops from a branch above the trail. Leaves and hanging vines hide the cat until it is already moving.
A fallen root mass shifts where no wind reaches it. The snake within waits for a bootstep close enough to strike from the wet leaves.
Branches crack overhead as angry apes hurl stones from the canopy. They beat the trunks and close in only if the party refuses to retreat.
Every direction looks green, wet, and close. Finding the old game trail through the hanging vines saves hours of hacking and noise.
Bright flowers burst into yellow dust when brushed. A quick breath and faster step keep the pollen from burning eyes and throats.
A broad-leafed plant holds clean rainwater high above the mud. The cool water eases heat-sickness and washes away the worst of the day's grime.
A stone idol's face peers from behind strangling vines, one eye recently scraped clean. Someone has been visiting it, and the trail leads deeper in.
A bright compass hangs from a branch above a troop of chattering monkeys. Recovering it means bargaining, climbing, or enduring a rain of fruit.
Thin smoke rises from a damp shelter where a scout has scratched directions into bark. The markings point toward medicine, danger, or both.
Heavy green fruit hangs above a slick trunk wrapped in vines. Cutting it loose cleanly feeds the party without dropping half the crop into the mud.
A red orchid grows from a rotten log, its root prized for bitter teas. The root must be lifted whole before the log collapses.
Small figs ripen where sunlight breaks through the canopy. Birds have taken many, but the shaded fruit is still sweet and dry.
A brown creek curls beneath fern banks, full of fish that vanish into silt at the first heavy step. A quiet net matters more than strength.
Prawns shelter among tangled roots where fresh water meets swampy pools. They can be swept into a basket before the mud clouds.
Eels coil in a flooded tree hollow left by last night's rain. Reaching them requires a steady hand and no fear of what else may be inside.
Smooth green stones show in the bend of a jungle creek. Sorting useful chunks from slippery river rock takes time before rain raises the water.
A bank of clean red clay rises behind a screen of ferns. It is easy to dig but quick to slump if the roots are cut wrong.
A quiet pool below a small fall flashes with gold when sunlight reaches the bottom. Panning it requires patience while biting insects gather.
A massive hardwood has fallen across a ravine, its core still dry beneath the wet bark. Cutting usable sections means controlling the roll.
A stand of straight bamboo clacks together in the humid breeze. The best poles can be taken quickly if the cutting line stays clean.
Amber resin beads from a storm-scarred trunk. The wood around the scar burns hot and fragrant if cut without wasting the living tree.
